title: You Can Have Your Ashes Turned Into a Playable Vinyl Record, When Your Day Comes
description:
in Life, Music |   	May 4th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Even in death we are only lim┬Łit┬Łed by our imag┬Łi┬Łna┬Łtion in how we want to go out. The
body:
in Life, Music |   	May 4th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Even in death we are only lim┬Łit┬Łed by our imag┬Łi┬Łna┬Łtion in how we want to go out. There are now ways to turn our corpse into a tree, or have our ash┬Łes shot into space, or press our ash┬Łes into dia┬ŁmondsŌĆōI believe Super┬Łman is involved in that last one. And now for the music lover, a com┬Łpa┬Łny called And Viny┬Łly will press your ash┬Łes into a playable vinyl record.
You like that pun┬Łny com┬Łpa┬Łny name? ThereŌĆÖs more: the busi┬Łness lets the dear depart┬Łed ŌĆ£Live on from beyond the groove.ŌĆØ Hear that groan? ThatŌĆÖs the deceased lit┬Łer┬Łal┬Łly spin┬Łning in their graveŌĆ”on a turntable.
The UK-based com┬Łpa┬Łny has been around since 2009, when Jason Leach launched it ŌĆ£just for funŌĆØ at first. But a lot of peo┬Łple liked the idea and have kept him in busi┬Łness.
It will cost, how┬Łev┬Łer. The basic ser┬Łvice gen┬Łer┬Łal┬Łly costs between ┬Ż1000 and ┬Ż3000 GBP, and it part┬Łly depends on how many vinyl records you pro┬Łduce. From what we can tell, you can┬Łnot use copy┬Łright-pro┬Łtect┬Łed music to fill up the 18ŌĆō22 min┬Łutes per side. So no ŌĆ£Free BirdŌĆØ or ŌĆ£We Are the Cham┬Łpi┬Łons,ŌĆØ unfor┬Łtu┬Łnate┬Łly. But you can put any┬Łthing else: a voice record┬Łing, or the sounds of nature, or com┬Łplete silence. Get more infor┬Łma┬Łtion over at the com┬Łpa┬ŁnyŌĆÖs FAQ.
No doubt, the ser┬Łvice can pro┬Łvide com┬Łfort and a mem┬Ło┬Łry trig┬Łger for those left behind. The above video, ŌĆ£Hear┬Łing Madge,ŌĆØ is a short doc about a son who took record┬Łings of his moth┬Łer and used And Viny┬Łly to make a record out of them. ItŌĆÖs sweet.
ŌĆ£IŌĆÖm sure a lot of peo┬Łple think that itŌĆÖs creepy, a lot of peo┬Łple think itŌĆÖs sac┬Łri┬Łle┬Łgious,ŌĆØ the man says. ŌĆ£But I know my moth┬Łer wouldnŌĆÖt have. She wouldŌĆÖve thought it was a hoot.ŌĆØ
Jason Leach, a musi┬Łcian and vinyl col┬Łlec┬Łtor him┬Łself, talks of the imme┬Łdi┬Ła┬Łcy of sound and what it means to many.
ŌĆ£Sound is vibrat┬Łing you, the room, and itŌĆÖs actu┬Łal┬Łly mov┬Łing the air around you,ŌĆØ he says. ŌĆ£And thatŌĆÖs whatŌĆÖs so pow┬Łer┬Łful about hear┬Łing someoneŌĆÖs voice on a record. TheyŌĆÖre actu┬Łal┬Łly mov┬Łing the air and for me thatŌĆÖs pow┬Łer┬Łful.ŌĆØ
Note: An ear┬Łli┬Łer ver┬Łsion of this post appeared on our site in 2017.
Relat┬Łed Con┬Łtent:
John CleeseŌĆÖs Eulo┬Łgy for Gra┬Łham Chap┬Łman: ŌĆśGood Rid┬Łdance, the Free-Load┬Łing Bas┬Łtard, I Hope He FriesŌĆÖ
Bronze Age Britons Turned Bones of Dead Rel┬Ła┬Łtives into Musi┬Łcal Instru┬Łments & Orna┬Łments
Watch Carl SaganŌĆÖs ŌĆ£A Glo┬Łri┬Łous DawnŌĆØ Become the First Vinyl Record Played in Space, Cour┬Łtesy of Jack White
Death: A Free Online Phi┬Łlos┬Ło┬Łphy Course from Yale Helps You Grap┬Łple with the Inescapable
 Ted Mills is a free┬Łlance writer on the arts.

title: Chuck JonesŌĆÖ The Dot and the Line Celebrates Geometry & Hard Work: An Oscar-Winning Animation (1965)
description:
in Animation, Education, Film, Math |   	May 4th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The ani┬Łmat┬Łed short above, The Dot and the Line, direct┬Łed by the
body:
in Animation, Education, Film, Math |   	May 4th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The ani┬Łmat┬Łed short above, The Dot and the Line, direct┬Łed by the great Chuck Jones and nar┬Łrat┬Łed by Eng┬Łlish actor Robert Mor┬Łley, won an Oscar in 19656 for Best Ani┬Łmat┬Łed Short Film. Based on a book writ┬Łten┬Āby Nor┬Łton Juster,┬ĀŌĆ£The Dot and the LineŌĆØ tells the sto┬Łry of a romance between two geo┬Łmet┬Łric shapesŌĆötaking the arche┬Łtyp┬Łal nar┬Łra┬Łtive tra┬Łjec┬Łto┬Łry of boy meets girl, los┬Łes girl, wins girl in the end (find┬Łing him┬Łself along the way) and inject┬Łing it with some fas┬Łci┬Łnat┬Łing social com┬Łmen┬Łtary that still res┬Łonates almost fifty years lat┬Łer. One way of watch┬Łing ŌĆ£The Dot and the LineŌĆØ is as a ŌĆ£tri┬Łumph of the nerdŌĆØ sto┬Łry, where an anx┬Łious square (as in ŌĆ£uncoolŌĆØ) Line has to com┬Łpete with a hip┬Łster beat┬Łnik Squig┬Łgle of a rival for the affec┬Łtions of a flighty Dot.
The Line begins the film ŌĆ£stiff as a stickŌĆ” dull, con┬Łven┬Łtion┬Łal and repressedŌĆØ (as his love inter┬Łest says of him) in con┬Łtrast to the groovy Squig┬Łgle and his groovy bebop sound┬Łtrack. With the pos┬Łsi┬Łble sug┬Łges┬Łtion that this love trans┬Łgress┬Łes mid-cen┬Łtu┬Łry racial bound┬Łaries, the LineŌĆÖs friends dis┬Łap┬Łprove and tell him to give it up, since ŌĆ£they all look alike any┬Łway.ŌĆØ But the Line per┬Łsists in his fol┬Łly, indulging in some Wal┬Łter Mit┬Łty-like rever┬Łies of hero┬Łic endeav┬Łors that might win over his Dot. Final┬Łly, using ŌĆ£great self-con┬Łtrol,ŌĆØ he man┬Łages to bend him┬Łself into an angle, then anoth┬Łer, then a series of sim┬Łple, then very com┬Łplex, shapes, becom┬Łing, we might assume, some kind of math┬Łe┬Łmat┬Łi┬Łcal wiz. After refin┬Łing his tal┬Łents alone, he goes off to show them to Dot, who is ŌĆ£over┬ŁwhelmedŌĆØ and delight┬Łed and who ŌĆ£gig┬Łgles like a school┬Łgirl.ŌĆØ
Here the sub┬Łtext of the nerd-gets-the-girl sto┬Łry┬Łline man┬Łi┬Łfests a fair┬Łly con┬Łser┬Łv┬Ła┬Łtive cri┬Łtique of the ŌĆ£anar┬ŁchyŌĆØ of the Squig┬Łgle, whom the Dot comes to see as ŌĆ£undis┬Łci┬Łplined, grace┬Łless, coarseŌĆØ and oth┬Łer unflat┬Łter┬Łing adjec┬Łtives while the lineŌĆöwho pro┬Łclaimed to him┬Łself ear┬Łli┬Łer that ŌĆ£free┬Łdom is not a license for chaosŌĆØŌĆöis ŌĆ£daz┬Łzling, clever, mys┬Łte┬Łri┬Łous, ver┬Łsa┬Łtile, light, elo┬Łquent, pro┬Łfound, enig┬Łmat┬Łic, com┬Łplex, and com┬Łpelling.ŌĆØ I can almost imag┬Łine that George Will had a hand in the writ┬Łing, which is to say that itŌĆÖs enor┬Łmous┬Łly clever, and enor┬Łmous┬Łly invest┬Łed in the val┬Łues of self-con┬Łtrol, hard work, and dis┬Łci┬Łpline, and dis┬Łtrust┬Łful of spon┬Łtane┬Łity, free play, and gen┬Łer┬Łal groovi┬Łness. At the end of the film, our Dot and Line go off to live ŌĆ£if not hap┬Łpi┬Łly ever after, at least rea┬Łson┬Łably soŌĆØ in some cozy sub┬Łurb, no doubt. The moral of the sto┬Łry? ŌĆ£To the vec┬Łtor belong the spoils.ŌĆØ
If you would like to sup┬Łport the mis┬Łsion of Open Cul┬Łture, con┬Łsid┬Łer mak┬Łing┬Āa dona┬Łtion to our site. ItŌĆÖs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your con┬Łtri┬Łbu┬Łtions will help us con┬Łtin┬Łue pro┬Łvid┬Łing the best free cul┬Łtur┬Łal and edu┬Łca┬Łtion┬Łal mate┬Łri┬Łals to learn┬Łers every┬Łwhere. You can con┬Łtribute through Pay┬ŁPal, Patre┬Łon, and Ven┬Łmo (@openculture). Thanks!

Jour┬Łney to the Cen┬Łter of a Tri┬Łan┬Łgle: Watch the 1977 Dig┬Łi┬Łtal Ani┬Łma┬Łtion That Demys┬Łti┬Łfies Geom┬Łe┬Łtry
Josh Jones is a writer and musi┬Łcian based in Durham, NC.┬Ā

title: Gijs Van Vaerenbergh Gracefully Reimagines a 16th-Century Belgian Abbey Church in Steel
description: In the late 12th century, a nobleman named Count Gerard van Loon commissioned an abbey to serve as his final resting place. Over the next few deca
body:
In the late 12th century, a nobleman named Count Gerard van Loon commissioned an abbey to serve as his final resting place. Over the next few decades, amid plenty of political tumult, Herkenrode Abbey in Hasselt, Belgium, was converted to the first Cistercian convent for women. It was a site of pilgrimage from the 13th to the 15th centuries, and despite regional wars and economic uncertainty, it stayed the course. During the 16th century, it experienced its heyday thanks to the patronage of a figure named Prince Bishop Evrard van der Marck, seeing the addition of a Gothic church that brimmed with beautiful stained glass windows, textiles, paintings, and more.
The Eighty YearsŌĆÖ War paused HerkenrodeŌĆÖs prosperity, and once things stabilized again politically, the abbey experienced several decades of good fortune, although much of this wealth was spent on the abbessesŌĆÖ own acquisitions of property and art in a show of their prestige. But the paradigm-shifting Li├©ge Revolution, which coincided with the French Revolution, brought all of this crashing to a halt by 1796. The abbey complex was promptly sold and dismantled.

Throughout the 19th and 20th centuries, Heckenrode went through many other uses, including factories and even a series of private homes, before it was again acquired by a religious organization in the 1970s. While the original 16th-century abbey church no longer exists due to a devastating fire in 1826, the site remains one of the regionŌĆÖs most culturally significant. And Herita has been working to restore it. As part of a phased regeneration of the landmark and its park, an ethereal, life-size sculpture of the abbey titled CLAUSURA by Gijs Van Vaerenbergh has risen from the buildingŌĆÖs original footprint.
The studio, founded by Belgian designers Pieterjan Gijs and Arnout Van Vaerenbergh, conceived of CLAUSURA as ŌĆ£an artistic vision for the vanished heart of Herkenrode.ŌĆØ True to scale, the structure is made of slender steel rods that rise from the ground in an airy framework. The installation revolves around the idea of memory and sensation: rather than rebuilding the abbey to try to mirror what it may have looked like hundreds of years ago, the work nods to its past with an airy elegance. 
ŌĆ£The new volumes are transparent, allowing their silhouettes to blend seamlessly with the landscape in the background,ŌĆØ says a statement. ŌĆ£The intervention balances between reconstruction and abstraction, as the original structures are evoked through a refined play of suggestion. Iconic details such as windows, vaults, and towers enhance the sense of recognisability, although at times, these elements dissolve back into a chaos of lines.ŌĆØ

Gijs Van Vaerenbergh is known for its architectural interventions, often utilizing steel, stone, wood, and a wide range of other building materials to re-envision spaces as structural sculptures. ŌĆ£What unites their diverse output is a sustained focus on┬Āhow space is experiencedŌĆövisually, bodily and temporally,ŌĆØ a statement says.
CLAUSURA is being constructed in three phases. The first, which is also the most ambitious, is slated to open to the public on June 18. Visitors will be able to walk and reflect amid the installation. See more on Gijs Van VaerenberghŌĆÖs Instagram, and learn about the restoration progress and how to visit on HeritaŌĆÖs website.

title: Vibrant Victorian-Era Transparencies Illuminate a Host of Microscopic Creatures
description: During the Victorian era, innovators made huge leaps with optical technologies. It was the period of the stereoscope and an early projector known
body:
During the Victorian era, innovators made huge leaps with optical technologies. It was the period of the stereoscope and an early projector known as the magic lantern, not to mention one in which eyeglasses became more affordable and entering the mainstream. These advances also influenced scientific inquiry, making microscopes more powerful, and the pursuit of microscopy enabled researchers and enthusiasts to discover creatures invisible to the naked eye.
One of these enthusiasts was London-based educator and amateur scientist Charles Thomas Hudson. Along with other scholars and aficionados, he participated in interest groups. ŌĆ£As President of the Royal Microscopical Society and a Fellow of the Royal Society, Hudson was a leading figure in this growing scientific community,ŌĆØ says a statement from Osh Gallery, which is currently exhibiting a collection of unique illustrations in The Hudson Transparencies.
Sea slugs
Curated by┬ĀLuke Powell and Jody Hudson-Powell of design firm Pentagram, which runs Osh Gallery, the exhibition literally brings to light a number of colorful transparencies that Hudson used during his lectures. While they appear dark and even unfinished in a typical setting, ŌĆ£when lit from behind these intricate works transform into magical visions of life previously only glimpsed when viewed under a microscope,ŌĆØ the gallery says.
The Hudson Transparencies includes 58 original transparencies that measure a surprisingly large 37.8 by 29.5 inches. Each of the graphicsŌĆÖ proportions are ŌĆ£the equivalent of drawing ants the size of elephants,ŌĆØ says a statement. The animals and botanicals emerge through a combination of painted paper and perforations, which are made with lines and clusters of pinholes.
Hudson was particularly fascinated by rotifers, a phylum of zooplankton named for their so-called ŌĆ£wheel-bearingŌĆØ characteristics. He also catalogued algae, protozoa, and larvaeŌĆösuch as that of the mayfly with its feather-like tail. Microscopic marine organisms were a particular favorite, though, and these back-lit images highlight the convergence of science and spectacular visuals that not only brought these creatures to life in a unique way for 19th-century viewers but continue to awe us today.
The Hudson Transparencies continues through June 11 in London. You might also enjoy Martin KunzŌĆÖs turn-of-the-century tactile graphics and Lorenz OkenŌĆÖs seminal natural history work,┬ĀAllgemaine Naturgeschichte F├╝r Alle St├żnde.
Opercularia nutans
A larva of a mayfly
A collection of rotifer species
Testudinella patina
Sponges, Porifera
Proales werneckii
Cupelopagis vorax

title: Met Gala Boycott Message Projected on BezosŌĆÖs Manhattan Penthouse
description:
News
                "All working-class people deserve better than what we're getting,ŌĆØ a former Amazon worker told Hyperallergi
body:
News
                "All working-class people deserve better than what we're getting,ŌĆØ a former Amazon worker told Hyperallergic ahead of this yearŌĆÖs gala, co-chaired by Jeff Bezos.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            A guerrilla projection on Jeff Bezos's luxury Madison Square Park residence on May 3, 2026 (all photos Isa Farfan/Hyperallergic)On the eve of the Met Gala in New York City, a series of messages condemning Jeff Bezos and Amazon lit up the billionaire's Madison Square Park luxury residence on Sunday, May 3.The guerrilla projection is the latest in the group Everyone Hates Elon's protest campaign against Bezos's involvement in Monday's event. After the Costume Institute at the Metropolitan Museum of Art named Bezos and his wife Lauren Sachez honorary co-chairs of the gala in February, the group has denounced Amazon's alleged dangerous workplace conditions and the company's lucrative contracts with federal immigration agencies through a series of visual actions.The group hid "piss bottles" in a Bezos protest ahead of the Met Gala. (screenshot via @everyonehateselon_ on Instagram)Last week, the group littered the Met with bottles of fake urine in a reference to claims from Amazon delivery workers that they were forced to pee in plastic bottles under severe time constraints.  The group also wheatpasted posters across the city that called for a boycott of the event. A video cast onto Bezos's penthouse on Sunday evening featured the testimony of Mary Hill, a 72-year-old Amazon warehouse worker who says she struggles to live paycheck to paycheck while battling cancer. Hill has been at the forefront of a campaign to secure better conditions for elder Amazon employees. In the video projection, she called for the Met Gala to honor the company's workers instead of the billionaire. "These are the people who should be honored at big events, not people who avoid their taxes and help Donald Trump,ŌĆØ an organizer for Everyone Hates Elon told Hyperallergic.A testimony by Amazon warehouse employee Mary Hill played against the exterior of the luxury building where Bezos's owns a penthouse Everyone Hates Elon also projected slogans, including "Boycott The Bezos Met Gala," onto the residence and, later, onto the Chrysler and Empire State buildings."ŌĆŖThe stories [the Amazon workers] have are incredibly powerful, and these are the people we should be celebrating, billionaires," an organizer for Everyone Hates Elon told Hyperallergic. "These are the people who should be honored at big events, not people who avoid their taxes and help Donald Trump." Passersby sit near Bezos's apartment building.Nearby, a small group of union workers, including Lamont Hopewell, who formerly worked in an Amazon warehouse in Queens, gathered to commemorate the projection. ŌĆ£All working-class people deserve better than what we're getting, especially workers at Amazon; that's the main reason we're here," Hopewell told Hyperallergic. Hopewell learned of the projection through his union, Teamsters Local 804.ŌĆŖ"It makes me feel inspired. It makes me feel like what we're doing is the right thing," Hopewell said, reacting to the display. "People are actually on board with us, even if they're not part of the organization; they still feel the same sentiment that we have."

                        Met Gala

                        Jeff Bezos

title: IŌĆÖve Got the Post-Duchamp Blues
description:
Art Review
                MoMAŌĆÖs Marcel Duchamp show made me long for those simpler times when "eliminating the artist's hand"
body:
Art Review
                MoMAŌĆÖs Marcel Duchamp show made me long for those simpler times when "eliminating the artist's hand" provided a pathway back to the true self.  
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            New York subway commuters passing by an ad for a Marcel Duchamp exhibition at the Museum of Modern Art (all photos Hakim Bishara/Hyperallergic) 
    
        
            In 1917, Marcel Duchamp turned a urinal on its head and called it art. With that, he earned his place in the canon as the great usurper of artistic norms, the enemy of yesteryearŌĆÖs tastemakers, the banisher of everything that came before him, and the godfather of art provocateurs.┬ĀWhat is art? After Duchamp, the answer became: ItŌĆÖs whatever you want it to be.A century later, weŌĆÖve kissed our attention spans goodbye, welcomed microplastics into our bloodstreams, and surrendered authorship over almost everything to AI ŌĆö from cake recipes to war target lists. The line between god-made and readymade barely exists anymore. The author today is not just ŌĆ£dead,ŌĆØ but dead on arrival.┬ĀWith the first Duchamp retrospective since 1973 at the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) in New York, a question arises: What does it mean to look at a ŌĆ£readymadeŌĆØ in 2026?A replica of Marcel Duchamp's famous "Fountain" (1917) (the original was lost) Featuring around 300 works from the Dadaist masterŌĆÖs six-decade career, the expansive exhibition was co-curated by MoMAŌĆÖs Ann Temkin and Michelle Kuo along with Matthew Affron from the Philadelphia Museum of Art, where it will travel next. The latter institution has the world's largest collection of Duchamp works, in large part thanks to a 1950 bequest by steel heir and poet Walter Conrad Arensberg.┬Ā┬Ā┬ĀWalking among the French-American artist's found urinals, bicycle wheels, snow shovels, and hat racks, I felt suddenly awash with mellow nostalgia.I noticed myself longing for those simpler times when "eliminating the artist's hand" from art-making ŌĆö what some have called ŌĆ£deskillingŌĆØ or ŌĆ£de-artingŌĆØ ŌĆö was supposed to provide a pathway back to the true self. Back then, freedom was the end and art was the means. Calling a urinal art was another way to break the chains of society.One of Duchamp's bicycle wheels From or by Marcel Duchamp or Rrose S├®lavy, ŌĆ£Box in a ValiseŌĆØ (1935ŌĆō1941)These days, however, relinquishing authorship means entering a hellscape of technological surveillance, unemployment, lack of agency and empathy, and environmental harm. We so dearly want to be freed from the constraints of being but find ourselves enslaved by the very tools that were meant to liberate us. (Head out to the reopened New Museum on the Lower East Side if you're in the mood for "posthuman" art. Personally, IŌĆÖm still stuck in the ŌĆ£human" stage of my development.)The MoMA showŌĆÖs biggest surprise and gift lies not in DuchampŌĆÖs groundbreaking readymades, which may feel quaint in 2026, but in his very much handmade oil-on-canvas paintings. Organized chronologically, the exhibition begins with a generous number of them, so many that I began to wonder if I was in the right place. "Nude Descending a Staircase, No. 2" (1912), which ruffled feathers at the Armory Show in New York the year after, pulled me in right away. It delivers what its title promises, except that the nude female figure is splintered beyond recognition, as if her movement was captured frame by frame by a slow-exposure camera. It is a living, breathing painting that still vibrates with energy more than a century after it was made. Standing before it was an unforgettable experience.┬ĀMarcel Duchamp, "Nude Descending a Staircase, No. 2" (1912) Like history itself, the figure never stops moving. She barrels through two world wars, the splitting of the atom, the hippies and beatniks; jazz, punk, and the blues; the fall of the Berlin Wall; the advent of the personal computer; the internet, and ultimately, AI. She will move within you, too, if you still consider yourself human.Marcel Duchamp continues at the Museum of Modern Art (11┬ĀWest 53┬ĀStreet, Manhattan) through August 22. The exhibition was curated by Ann Temkin, Michelle Kuo, and Matthew Affron with Alexandra ŌĆ£LoŌĆØ Drexelius, Helena Klevorn, Danielle Cooke, and Julia V├Īzquez. 
                  
                      
                        Art Review
                      
                      
                        Review
                      
                      
                        Marcel Duchamp
                      
                      
                        MoMA
                      
                      
                        Louisiana Museum of Modern Art
                      
                      
                        New York

title: Artists, Read the Fine Print
description:
Daily Newsletter
                The insidiousness of artists' contacts, visitors will now judge the Venice Biennale, and TrumpŌĆÖ
body:
Daily Newsletter
                The insidiousness of artists' contacts, visitors will now judge the Venice Biennale, and TrumpŌĆÖs border crew damages 1,000-year-old Native etching. 
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            ŌĆ£The art world runs on informality,ŌĆØ artist Damien Davis writes, ŌĆ£until informality stops serving the institution.ŌĆØ In todayŌĆÖs must-read piece, Davis takes on the many ways so-called ŌĆ£standardŌĆØ contracts actually undermine artistsŌĆÖ power. The cost of pushing back feels higher than the risk of signing, he writes, and sometimes, you canŌĆÖt afford to walk away.Speaking of walking away: The latest twist in this yearŌĆÖs Venice Biennale, after the awards jury resigned last week, is that you are now its judge. There will be no Golden Lions, the eventŌĆÖs top prize; instead, ticket holders will vote on ŌĆ£Visitor Lions,ŌĆØ with awards announced when the show closes in November. Conveniently, Israel and Russia are eligible after the jury said it would ban them from consideration due to charges of crimes against humanity. Okay, little lion, letŌĆÖs hear that roar.┬ĀŌĆö Lisa Yin Zhang, associate editor
Artists, read carefully before you sign. (illustration by Shari Flores/Hyperallergic) What Artists Sign AwayLong consignment periods, moral rights waivers, and opaque ŌĆ£standardŌĆØ contracts serve the institution more than the artist. | Damien Davis
